meta.title: Atlantic Crossing
meta.author: Troy Wolff
meta.focusQuestion: How does Patrick Kelley's journey from Ireland to America change him?

sections.title: Leaving Dublin Bay
paragraphs: <s>Patrick Kelley</s> <v>looked out over</v> <o>the ships floating in Dublin Bay</o>. <s>Below him</s>, <v>he saw</v> <o>hundreds of people crowding the docks</o>, looking for space on one of the ships bound for America. The year was 1846, and many Irish people, most of whom were farmers, were starving. For the past two years, <s>Ireland's potato crop</s> <v>had failed</v> due to disease, <v>resulting</v> in <o>a great famine</o>.

To avoid starvation, <s>Irish families like Patrick's</s> <v>bought</v> <o>passage on ships of all sizes sailing to the United States and Canada</o>. The journey was dangerous, but <s>the promise of a better life in America</s>, where there was more land to farm and no diseases destroying the crops, <v>drew</v> <o>many</o> to the harbor. <s>Patrick</s> <v>heard</v> <o>his father talk of jobs in big cities like New York and Boston</o>. <s>His father, who was a blacksmith,</s> <v>hoped</v> <o>to find a job working his craft in New York</o>, which had thousands of horses to pull carriages and wagons through the streets.

<s>Patrick</s> <v>knew</v> <o>his family was suffering</o>, and he knew that America might offer a better life, yet Patrick did not want to leave Dublin. This was his home. <s>He</s> <v>did not want to leave</v> <o>his friends, despite the food shortage</o>, and he did not want to leave Dublin Bay, his favorite place. <s>He</s> often <v>sat</v> <o>on the shore overlooking the bay during storms</o> and felt the sea spray in his face, and he would listen to the stories of the suntanned sailors returning from journeys all around the world.

<s>Patrick</s> <v>wanted to be</v> <o>a sailor in the warm South Pacific Ocean</o>; he did not want to be a crowded passenger aboard an immigrant ship crossing the cold Atlantic. So, <s>Patrick</s> <v>sat</v> <o>on the docks by himself</o>, angry with his family, mostly his father, for making him leave. <s>He</s> <v>shaded</v> <o>his eyes from the setting sun on the horizon</o> and looked for the tall mast of the Donegal, the ship that would take his family to America the following morning. <s>He</s> <v>spotted</v> <o>the Donegal a little farther down the docks</o>; it was easy to pick out because it was larger than most of the other ships. <s>He</s> <v>could see</v> <o>the name painted in gold letters across the stern of the ship</o> as it gently bobbed in the water. <s>Patrick</s> <v>glared at</v> <o>the massive boat</o>, wishing it would sink to the bottom of Dublin Bay.

sections.title: Promise of a Better Life
paragraphs: By the time Patrick finally decided to go home, it had grown dark. Patrick lived on the edge of Dublin, where his house was connected to his father's large blacksmith barn and shop. After he left the edge of the water, the streets and alleyways leading home were so dark that he could see just a short way ahead.

After a few blocks, <s>Patrick</s> <v>noticed</v> <o>footsteps behind him</o>. The streets were usually deserted after dark, and Patrick wondered who might be out tonight. The footsteps drew closer and closer, and as he listened, <s>Patrick</s> <v>detected</v> <o>a strange metallic clicking along with the footsteps</o>. Frightened, <s>Patrick</s> <v>kept his head down and began walking faster</v>.

Another block later, the steps and the clicking got closer still. Frantically, <s>Patrick</s> <v>tried to remember</v> <o>an alleyway or doorway he could duck into</o> to get away from this stranger following him. Suddenly, <s>Patrick</s> <v>felt</v> <o>a small jab on the top of his shoulder</o>. His blood froze in terror. Still frightened, Patrick slowly turned around. Standing over him <v>glared</v> <s>an older man with a silver beard glowing in the lamplight and a military sword in his right hand</s>.
"Who are you?" Patrick asked, trying to sound confident. "I am First Mate Thomas O'Brien, an officer of the Donegal, at your service," he answered in a serious tone.

"But I hear the living conditions are bad on these ships," Patrick said. "Well, they often are," Thomas replied. "Some ships are worse than others. Unfortunately, conditions usually depend upon how much money a family can spend on their fare." "Your family will travel on a ship with a record for bringing its passengers safely to America, even though the journey will still be difficult and uncomfortable," O'Brien said.

Patrick thought about this for a moment. "I can't remember the last time I wasn't going to bed feeling hungry. I don't want to live like that much longer." "Exactly," Thomas responded. "In an emergency, people must make difficult choices to make life better. But listen to me, young man. I think that a better life is waiting for you and your family. You will have to work very hard, but if you do, there should be no limit to what you can accomplish."

"Of course he's right, son," Thomas answered. "Well, young man, it's getting late. You should be off to bed. After all, this may be the last peaceful night's sleep you'll have for several weeks," he said with a wink.

sections.title: Stormy Seas
paragraphs: A week later, Patrick and his family were well on their way to America aboard the Donegal. "All hands on deck! All hands on deck!" <s>Patrick</s> <v>heard</v> <o>from above</o>. In the darkness below decks, <s>Patrick</s> <v>heard</v> <o>his sister groaning</o>. She had been sick for three days, like many of the people on the ship. After several days of calm seas, the Donegal had run into a violent storm.

On the first day of the storm, Patrick watched from the deck with excitement as giant walls of water would swell and approach the ship. <s>Patrick's stomach</s> would <v>rise</v> in his throat as <s>the Donegal</s> <v>climbed</v>, <v>hovered</v> for one awful second at <o>the crest of the wave</o>, then <v>crashed down</v> the far side. This was exciting, but the storm was getting stronger.

"All hands on deck! We must lower these sails, boys!" Patrick heard these commands again from above. He knew that "hands" only meant the sailors, not any of the passengers, but he felt he couldn't stand staying below in the darkness one more moment. So, when no one was looking, <s>Patrick</s> <v>burst</v> up <o>the main hatch</o> and into the howling wind and freezing spray.

The deck of the ship was wildly confusing—sailors running around, officers barking orders, sails flapping in the wind, and white ocean spray crashing over the rails of the ship. "Excuse me, sir!" he said as he tugged on the captain's coat. <s>The captain</s> <v>turned quickly and yelled</v>, "You, boy! What are you doing above deck? Get yourself below!"

"Captain! <s>One of the sails</s> <v>has wrapped</v> <o>itself around the upper yardarm</o>!" a sailor yelled. "Well, send someone up there to untangle it! We need that sail down before the wind tears it!" the captain thundered. "We can't, sir," the sailor replied. "The yardarm has been damaged and can't support a sailor's weight. We would need someone much smaller."

"What? You? What makes you think you could do this?" "It's really no problem, sir. I've grown up along the coast all my life. <s>I</s> <v>have to climb</v> in <o>the rafters of my father's blacksmith barn</o> every day to hang up his tools." Patrick said this with as much confidence as he could fake, but the wild wind and water frightened him.
"All right, lad. Be careful, climb slowly, and do NOT look down. Just concentrate on the mast ahead of you. You can do this! Now, up you go."

Climbing the mast wasn't scary for Patrick at first, as long as he just looked straight ahead. Halfway up, however, <s>Patrick</s> <v>was unable to resist</v> <o>temptation</o> and looked out and below him. Through a crisscross of ropes whistling in the wind, he saw the white tops of the waves and the small heads of the sailors on deck. He felt a wave of dizziness overtake him. Fighting against this dizziness, <s>Patrick</s> <v>refocused on</v> <o>the mast in front of him</o>. His head quickly cleared, and he climbed the rest of the way up.

At the top, he could see the crack in the wood of the yardarm. Looking out, he could see the top of the sail flapping in the wind and the clip he needed to unhook. Carefully reaching out from the mast, <s>Patrick</s> <v>stretched</v> <o>his hand toward the clip</o>. Stretching a bit farther, feeling the mast sway dangerously under him, he caught hold of the clip. Tugging with all of the strength he could find, <s>he</s> <v>felt</v> <o>the clip give way and the sail drop below him</o>. <s>A cheer</s> <v>rose</v> from <o>the sailors below</o>, and <s>the captain</s> happily <v>waved</v> at him to come back down.

sections.title: Arriving in America
paragraphs: Three weeks later, Patrick stood on the deck of the Donegal with his family. Before them the buildings of New York City came into view. At last, they had made it to America! <s>Patrick</s> <v>could feel</v> <o>the energy and bustle of this new, growing city</o>, so different from the centuries-old calmness of Dublin.

"Why, Patrick, my boy!" <s>he</s> <v>heard</v> <o>a familiar voice behind him</o>. It was First Mate Thomas O'Brien. <s>Thomas</s> <v>came up to Patrick and put</v> <o>his hand on Patrick's shoulder</o>.

Looking at Patrick's father, <s>Thomas</s> <v>said</v>, "You know, Mr. Kelly, none of us would be here in New York right now if it weren't for your son here. What he did up on that mast in the storm was quite courageous. Quite remarkable."

"Well, <s>we</s> <v>are all very proud of</v> <o>Patrick</o>," <s>Mr. Kelly</s> <v>said</v>, looking fondly at his son. "And <s>we</s> <v>are also very thankful to</v> <o>you and the rest of the crew of the Donegal</o>. Thanks to you, my family now has a chance for a new life—a better life—here in America."

"I wish you all the luck in the world," Thomas answered. "And, Patrick, I hope to see you at the harbor whenever the Donegal is in port. <s>You</s> <v>have</v> <o>the makings of a fine sailor</o>, and you're welcome on the deck of this ship any time."
<s>Patrick</s> <v>smiled excitedly at this invitation and imagined</v> <o>his future</o>, sailing the oceans of the world, but always returning to his family and to America, his new home.

vocabulary.definition: someone who works with iron, including making horseshoes
vocabulary.sentence: <s>His father, who was a blacksmith,</s> <v>hoped</v> <o>to find a job working his craft in New York</o>.

options: a sailor who works on a ship and sails across the ocean
someone who works with iron, including making horseshoes and tools
a captain who commands a ship and guides it across the sea
a farmer who grows potatoes and other crops in the fields

vocabulary.sentence: <s>Patrick</s> <v>could feel</v> <o>the energy and bustle of this new, growing city</o>.

options: quiet and calm weather with no wind or sound at all
busy and noisy activity in a crowded and lively place
a type of ship sail that catches the wind to move forward
a violent storm at sea with large waves and strong winds

vocabulary.definition: highest point, or top of, something
vocabulary.sentence: <s>The Donegal</s> <v>climbed</v>, <v>hovered</v> for one awful second at <o>the crest of the wave</o>, then <v>crashed down</v>.

options: the bottom of the wave where it meets the deep ocean
the highest point, or top of, something such as a wave
the middle of the ocean far away from any shore or land
the shore of the beach where waves crash onto the sand

vocabulary.sentence: For the past two years, Ireland's potato crop had failed due to disease, resulting in <o>a great famine</o>.

options: a type of ship used to carry passengers across the ocean
a major food shortage that causes many people to go hungry
a violent storm at sea with very large and dangerous waves
a new country where people go to find a better life

vocabulary.sentence: Frantically, Patrick tried to remember an alleyway or doorway he could duck into.

options: calmly and slowly without any sense of worry or fear
wildly acting with strong emotion such as fear or panic
quietly and carefully so as not to be noticed by anyone
happily and excitedly with great joy and enthusiasm

vocabulary.definition: covering for the opening that leads from a ship's deck to the areas below
vocabulary.sentence: <s>Patrick</s> <v>burst</v> up <o>the main hatch</o> and into the howling wind and freezing spray.

options: a type of sail that catches the wind to move the ship
the covering for the opening from the deck to areas below
the front of the ship that cuts through the water as it moves
a large wave that crashes over the side of the ship

vocabulary.definition: person who has come to a new country to live
vocabulary.sentence: <s>Patrick</s> <v>did not want to be</v> <o>a crowded passenger aboard an immigrant ship crossing the cold Atlantic</o>.

options: a person traveling to a new place for a short vacation
a person who has come to a new country to live there permanently
a sailor who works on a ship and travels across the ocean
a captain of a vessel who guides the ship across the sea

vocabulary.definition: the qualities needed to do something

options: the tools and materials needed to build something physical
the qualities and abilities needed to do something well
the money needed to buy something expensive or valuable
the friends needed to help you when you are in trouble

vocabulary.definition: a tall pole made of wood that stands on the deck of a ship to support the sails
vocabulary.sentence: He shaded his eyes and looked for the tall mast of the Donegal.

options: the rear of a ship where the name is painted in gold
a tall pole on a ship's deck that supports the sails
a type of rope used to tie the ship to the dock
a large wave that crashes against the side of the ship

vocabulary.definition: wooden boards that support a roof
vocabulary.sentence: <s>I</s> <v>have to climb</v> in <o>the rafters of my father's blacksmith barn</o> every day to hang up his tools.

options: metal tools used for climbing up the side of a tall building
wooden boards that support a roof and form the frame above
parts of a ship's sail that catch the wind as it blows
types of knots used by sailors to tie ropes on the deck

vocabulary.definition: the rear end of a ship
vocabulary.sentence: <s>He</s> <v>could see</v> <o>the name painted in gold letters across the stern of the ship</o>.

options: the front of the ship that points in the direction of travel
the rear end of a ship where the name is often painted
the tallest mast that rises above the deck of the ship
the deepest part of the ship located below the main deck

vocabulary.definition: the outer tip of a horizontal beam supporting a square sail on a ship
vocabulary.sentence: <s>One of the sails</s> <v>has wrapped</v> <o>itself around the upper yardarm</o>!

options: a type of rope used to tie sails to the mast of the ship
the outer tip of a horizontal beam supporting a sail on a ship
the bottom of the ship that sits below the surface of the water
a large ocean wave that crashes over the deck during a storm

problemSolution.title: The Great Irish Famine
problemSolution.context: Ireland, 1846. The potato crop has failed for two years.

problem: Ireland's potato crop failed, causing a great famine and starvation
The Donegal ship had a hole
Patrick's father lost his blacksmith tools
The sailors refused to sail to America
cause: A disease destroyed the potato plants for two years
Too many people moved to America
effect: Irish families were starving and had to leave their homeland
solution: Irish families emigrated to America where there was more land and no crop diseases
They waited for the crops to grow back
They asked other countries for food

problemSolution.title: Patrick Doesn't Want to Leave
problemSolution.context: Patrick loves Dublin Bay and doesn't want to be an immigrant passenger.

problem: Patrick was angry about leaving Dublin, his friends, and his dream of being a South Pacific sailor
cause: He loved his home and felt he had no choice but to follow his family's decision
He was afraid of the ocean
effect: Patrick sat on the docks glaring at the ship, wishing it would sink
solution: First Mate O'Brien encouraged Patrick, explaining that difficult choices lead to a better life in America
The captain gave him a job
Patrick's friends convinced him to stay

problemSolution.title: The Storm and the Tangled Sail
problemSolution.context: The Donegal hits a violent Atlantic storm. A sail wraps around a damaged yardarm.

problem: A sail wrapped around the damaged upper yardarm and was about to tear, but no sailor was small enough to climb up
cause: The violent storm damaged the yardarm so it couldn't support an adult sailor's weight
The wind blew from the wrong direction
effect: The ship was in danger because the sail couldn't be lowered in the storm
solution: Patrick volunteered to climb the mast because he was small and light from climbing barn rafters every day
The crew cut the sail loose
They waited for the storm to pass

questions.question: Which country is Patrick's homeland?
questions.evidence: Patrick Kelley looked out over the ships floating in Dublin Bay...many Irish people were starving.
choices: England, where many people moved to find work during the famine
Ireland, where Patrick looked out over the ships in Dublin Bay
Australia, a faraway land across the ocean from Ireland
United States, where Patrick's family eventually decided to move to
questions.explanation: Patrick's homeland is Ireland — he looks out over Dublin Bay and is an Irish person facing the potato famine.

questions.question: Which of the following is true?
questions.evidence: The year was 1846, and many Irish people, most of whom were farmers, were starving. For the past two years, Ireland's potato crop had failed due to disease, resulting in a great famine.
choices: The potato famine of 1846 was not very bad and people had plenty of food.
Patrick's family did not eat potatoes and so they were not affected by the famine.
There would be no food for Patrick's family in the United States when they arrived.
Patrick's family might have starved to death if they had not left their homeland.
questions.explanation: The potato famine was severe — Patrick's family faced starvation, which is why they had to leave Ireland.

questions.question: What is the author's purpose for having Patrick meet First Mate Thomas O'Brien?
questions.evidence: I think that a better life is waiting for you and your family.
choices: To let the reader know what traveling aboard a ship was like and encourage Patrick
To scare Patrick and make him afraid of the journey across the ocean
To introduce a new character who helps Patrick feel afraid of the ship
To make Patrick want to become a sailor on the cold Atlantic Ocean
questions.explanation: The author uses this meeting to show what ship travel was like and to introduce a character who encourages Patrick.

questions.question: If Patrick is standing at the stern of the ship, where is he?
questions.evidence: He could see the name painted in gold letters across the stern of the ship.
choices: on the right side of the ship, looking out toward the open ocean
at the rear of the ship, where the name is painted in gold letters
at the front of the ship, where it cuts through the ocean waves
on the left side of the ship, facing the docks and the harbor
questions.explanation: The stern is the rear (back) of a ship.

questions.question: What is First Mate Thomas O'Brien's most important message to Patrick?
questions.evidence: I think that a better life is waiting for you and your family. You will have to work very hard, but if you do, there should be no limit to what you can accomplish.
choices: The voyage across the Atlantic Ocean will be very difficult and uncomfortable.
The Donegal is a good ship with a record for keeping passengers safe.
People who have a lot of money travel in better conditions on the ship.
Opportunities for a new and better life await Patrick and his family in America.
questions.explanation: O'Brien's message is that Patrick and his family can have a better life in America if they work hard.

questions.question: Why is Patrick so eager to watch the sailors handle the boat during the storm?
questions.evidence: Patrick wanted to be a sailor in the warm South Pacific Ocean.
choices: He likes to watch the large waves crashing against the side of the boat.
His sister is sick below deck and he wants to help the sailors heal her.
He wants to be a sailor and is fascinated by how they work on the ship.
His dream is to be a hero who saves the ship from the violent Atlantic storm.
questions.explanation: Patrick dreams of being a sailor, which is why he's fascinated by watching them work.

questions.question: Why does the Captain allow Patrick to climb the mast?
questions.evidence: The yardarm has been damaged and can't support a sailor's weight. We would need someone much smaller.
choices: All of the sailors have other important duties and cannot stop what they are doing.
Patrick does not weigh as much as the grown men and can climb safely up there.
The Captain wants him to get out of the way and go somewhere else on the ship.
The Captain does not allow Patrick to climb the mast and tells him to go below.
questions.explanation: The damaged yardarm can't support an adult's weight, so Patrick, who is smaller and lighter, is the only one who can climb up.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains how Patrick felt while climbing the mast?
questions.evidence: but the wild wind and water frightened him...He felt a wave of dizziness overtake him.
choices: happy and scared at the same time as he climbed higher up the tall mast
scared and sad because he missed his home and his friends in Dublin
happy and sad because he was leaving Ireland but excited about America
scared and excited because he was frightened but determined to help the crew
questions.explanation: Patrick was scared (frightened by wind and water, dizzy) but also excited to help and prove himself.

questions.question: What is the name of the ship that will take Patrick and his family to America?
questions.evidence: He shaded his eyes from the setting sun on the horizon and looked for the tall mast of the Donegal.
choices: The Dublin Bay, a large harbor where many ships floated
The Atlantic, the cold ocean they would cross to reach America
The Donegal, a large ship bigger than most others at the docks
The O'Brien, named after the First Mate who guided the ship
questions.explanation: The ship's name is the Donegal.

questions.question: Which word best completes the sentence? 'The student has the ___ of a scientist.'
questions.evidence: You have the makings of a fine sailor.
choices: immigrant, because the student came from a faraway country to study
bustle, because the student was always busy and noisy in the classroom
crest, because the student reached the highest point of the class
makings, because the student has the qualities needed to become a scientist
questions.explanation: 'Makings' means the qualities needed to do something — 'has the makings of' means has the qualities needed to become.

questions.question: Imagine you are Patrick and have just climbed the mast to free the sail. Write a journal entry about what it was like and how you felt afterward.
questions.explanation: This is a creative writing prompt. Think about what Patrick felt: fear, dizziness, determination, pride.
opinionPrompts: How did Patrick feel climbing the mast?
How did he feel after the sailors cheered?

questions.question: Why does Patrick's family think they would have a chance at a better life in America?
questions.evidence: The promise of a better life in America, where there was more land to farm and no diseases destroying the crops, drew many to the harbor.
questions.explanation: America offered more farmland, no potato disease, and job opportunities for Patrick's father as a blacksmith.
opinionPrompts: What made America seem better than Ireland?
What did Patrick's father hope to find?

ch1.title: Famine
ch1.content: A major food shortage — many people go hungry or starve. Ireland's potato crop failed for TWO years!

ch1.title: Immigrant
ch1.content: A person who comes to a new country to LIVE there permanently. Patrick's family are immigrants.

ch2.title: Frantically
ch2.content: Acting wildly with emotion — very fast and scared. Patrick walked frantically when he heard footsteps behind him!

ch2.title: Possessive Nouns
ch2.content: Ireland's potato famine — the apostrophe-s shows the famine BELONGS to Ireland. 'Farmers' crops' = apostrophe after the s for plural possessive.

ch3.title: Mast
ch3.content: The tall wooden pole on a ship that holds the sails. Patrick climbed the mast during the storm!

ch3.title: Yardarm
ch3.content: The horizontal beam that supports a sail. When it cracked, no adult sailor was light enough to climb up and fix it.

ch3.title: Possessive Nouns
ch3.content: The captain's coat = singular possessive (one captain). The sailors' commands = plural possessive (many sailors). Can you find more in Chapter 3?

ch4.title: Bustle
ch4.content: Busy and noisy activity. New York City had so much bustle — very different from quiet Dublin!

ch4.title: Makings
ch4.content: The qualities needed to do something. Thomas said Patrick has the makings of a fine sailor!

ch4.title: Problem & Solution
ch4.content: Every good story has problems and solutions. Can you identify all THREE problems Patrick faced and how each was solved?
